description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental map of the entire RLHF pipeline before diving into details, showing how each phase builds on the previous one and where RLHF fits relative to pretraining and SFT
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)

description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of token-by-token language model completion concrete and intuitive, helping students viscerally understand how LLMs generate text before learning how RLHF modifies that behavior
when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
suggested_caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens
teaching_value: Concretizes the scale of pretraining data (1T tokens) and illustrates the diversity of data sources used, helping students understand the data bottleneck problem and why data sourcing decisions matter for model behavior
when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
suggested_caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)

alt: We're at the risk of running out of Internet data
description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the looming data scarcity problem for LLM pretraining, motivating why data efficiency and alternative training signals (like human feedback) are increasingly important research directions
when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
suggested_caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)

description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT
teaching_value: Concretely shows why raw pretraining completion is insufficient for user-facing applications — the model may produce valid but unhelpful completions — making the need for SFT immediately intuitive
when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
suggested_caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields
teaching_value: Illustrates that human feedback quality depends on who the labelers are, prompting students to think critically about whose values and preferences are encoded in RLHF-trained models and the subjectivity of 'good' responses
when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
suggested_caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases
teaching_value: Clarifies the architectural and conceptual shift from imitation learning in SFT to reward-maximization in RLHF, helping students understand why a reward model is needed and how the training loop changes
when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
suggested_caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective
teaching_value: Translates the mathematical loss function into an intuitive visual, helping students understand that the RM is trained to maximize the score gap between preferred and rejected responses rather than predict absolute scores
when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with some math background who benefit from seeing loss landscape behavior
suggested_caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot of OpenAI's internal labeling UI used to collect InstructGPT reward model training data, showing a prompt with multiple model responses that labelers score on a 1–7 scale and rank by preference
teaching_value: Makes the abstract data collection process for RLHF concrete by showing the actual interface labelers used, helping students understand how pairwise comparison data is gathered and what inter-labeler agreement means in practice
when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the practical challenges of scaling human feedback and the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic
suggested_caption: OpenAI's InstructGPT labeling UI: labelers rank responses to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)

description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.
teaching_value: Gives students a high-level mental map of the entire RLHF pipeline before diving into details, making it easier to understand where each component fits and why the KL divergence constraint is needed in phase 3.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
suggested_caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)

description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing the combined optimization target as a sum of the expected reward from the reward model minus a KL divergence penalty term that keeps the RL policy close to the SFT model.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract trade-off between maximizing reward and staying close to the SFT baseline concrete and precise, helping students understand why the KL term prevents reward hacking and model drift.
when_to_show: Introduce after students understand the three-phase pipeline; use when explaining why the RL fine-tuning phase needs a constraint and how that constraint is mathematically encoded.
suggested_caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT. (huyenchip.com)

description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.
teaching_value: Illustrates the theoretical motivation behind using RLHF to address hallucination, setting up the important contrast with the empirical finding that RLHF actually worsened hallucination in practice.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the limitations and unintended consequences of RLHF, specifically to set up the tension between Schulman's theoretical proposal and the InstructGPT empirical results.
suggested_caption: Schulman's proposal: use RL to force LLMs to admit uncertainty. (huyenchip.com)

description: A chart or figure from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit higher hallucination rates compared to SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence of a key unintended side-effect of RLHF, teaching students that optimizing for human preference does not uniformly improve all model properties and that hallucination is a persistent challenge.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the Schulman hallucination-fix slide to highlight the gap between theory and practice, and when discussing evaluation metrics and the limitations of human feedback as a training signal.
suggested_caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF improves preference but worsens hallucination. (huyenchip.com)

alt: Context needed for a customer support query
description: A diagram illustrating the multiple types of context required to answer a customer support query, showing how user history, product information, and situational factors feed into an LLM response
teaching_value: Helps students visualize why raw LLM queries are insufficient for enterprise use cases and what dimensions of context must be retrieved or injected to produce accurate answers
when_to_show: Introduce this when teaching why vanilla LLMs fail in production settings and before explaining RAG or prompt engineering strategies
suggested_caption: Multiple context types needed for a single customer support query. (Huyenchip, 2023)

alt: Context needed for a customer support query
description: A screenshot from Jerry Liu's LlamaIndex talk showing Phase 1 of RAG: the chunking/indexing pipeline, illustrating how documents are split into chunks and stored in a vector index for later retrieval
teaching_value: Concretely shows students the first stage of a RAG pipeline — chunking strategy and indexing — making abstract retrieval concepts tangible with a real framework example
when_to_show: Show when introducing RAG architecture, specifically when explaining the offline indexing phase before discussing retrieval and generation phases
suggested_caption: RAG Phase 1: chunking and indexing documents into a vector store. (Liu/LlamaIndex, 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of DeepMind's Flamingo multimodal model, showing how visual features from a vision encoder are interleaved with text tokens and fed into a large language model via cross-attention layers
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete architectural blueprint for how vision and language modalities are fused in a state-of-the-art multimodal LLM, illustrating cross-attention as the bridging mechanism
when_to_show: Use when teaching multimodal model design, especially to contrast with text-only transformer architectures and explain how non-text modalities are integrated
suggested_caption: Flamingo's architecture fuses visual and language features via cross-attention. (DeepMind)

alt: Guanaco 7B's performance compared to ChatGPT GPT-3.5 and GPT-4
description: A bar chart comparing Guanaco 7B's performance against ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) and GPT-4 on benchmark tasks, demonstrating that a small, optimized open-source model can approach proprietary model performance
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that model compression and efficient fine-tuning (QLoRA) can yield competitive performance at a fraction of the compute cost, motivating study of optimization techniques
when_to_show: Show when discussing the cost-performance tradeoff in LLM deployment and when introducing model optimization techniques like quantization and LoRA
suggested_caption: Guanaco 7B approaches GPT-4 performance via QLoRA compression. (Dettmers et al., 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of Monarch Mixer, a non-Transformer model architecture using structured Monarch matrices for both sequence mixing and feature mixing, proposed as a hardware-efficient alternative to attention
teaching_value: Illustrates a concrete alternative to the Transformer attention mechanism, helping students understand the architectural design space beyond self-attention and the motivation for hardware-aware model design
when_to_show: Introduce when discussing limitations of the Transformer architecture (quadratic attention complexity) and when surveying next-generation model architectures
suggested_caption: Monarch Mixer replaces attention with structured Monarch matrix operations. (Fu et al., 2023)

alt: Timeline of advances of the three major methods in photonic matrix multiplication
description: A timeline chart showing advances across three major methods in photonic matrix multiplication over time, illustrating the rapid progress in photonic computing as a potential GPU alternative for AI workloads
teaching_value: Contextualizes the hardware innovation landscape for students, showing that compute bottlenecks are being attacked from a physics level (photons vs electrons) and situating photonics within the broader AI hardware race
when_to_show: Use when teaching AI infrastructure and hardware constraints, particularly when discussing why new chip architectures are needed to scale LLMs cost-effectively
suggested_caption: Timeline of photonic matrix multiplication advances across three key methods. (Huyenchip, 2023)

description: A demographic breakdown chart of labelers used for InstructGPT's RLHF training data, showing age distribution, geographic origin (predominantly Filipino and Bangladeshi), and other demographic attributes
teaching_value: Makes the demographic bias problem in RLHF training data concrete and measurable, helping students understand how 'human preference' is not universal but shaped by who the labelers are
when_to_show: Show when discussing RLHF and alignment, specifically to challenge the assumption that human feedback is neutral or representative of all users
suggested_caption: InstructGPT labelers: no one over 65, predominantly Filipino and Bangladeshi. (Ouyang et al., 2022)

alt: Self-reported demographics of contributors to OpenAssistant dataset
description: A self-reported demographics chart for contributors to the OpenAssistant dataset, showing that approximately 90.5% of the 222 respondents identify as male, with breakdowns of other demographic variables
teaching_value: Provides a stark quantitative example of gender imbalance in community-sourced AI training data, reinforcing lessons about how volunteer-driven datasets can systematically underrepresent large portions of the population
when_to_show: Pair with the InstructGPT demographics image when teaching data collection challenges in alignment; use to spark discussion on how to design more representative feedback collection
suggested_caption: OpenAssistant contributors: 90.5% male among 222 respondents. (OpenAssistant, 2023)

alt: Chat has been used as the universal interface for superapps in China for over a decade
description: A visual showing chat as the universal interface for Chinese super-apps (e.g., WeChat), demonstrating that conversational UI for complex multi-domain tasks has been deployed at scale in Asia for over a decade
teaching_value: Provides historical and cross-cultural context showing that the 'chat as interface' debate predates LLMs, helping students evaluate the chat paradigm with evidence rather than novelty bias
when_to_show: Use when discussing LLM interface design and the question of whether chat is the right modality, to ground the discussion in real-world precedent from Asian markets
suggested_caption: Chat as universal super-app interface in China — a decade of precedent. (Dan Grover, 2014)

description: A screenshot of NVIDIA's NeVA multimodal chatbot interface, showing a standard text-chat UI used to interact with a vision-language model, with an image input and text response displayed
teaching_value: Serves as a UX critique case study: illustrates that even powerful multimodal models are often wrapped in generic chat interfaces that don't leverage the visual modality effectively, motivating better interface design research
when_to_show: Use when discussing the gap between model capability and interface design in multimodal AI, prompting students to think about what a purpose-built multimodal UI should look like
suggested_caption: NVIDIA NeVA: powerful multimodal model, but generic chat interface. (NVIDIA, 2023)

description: A comparison of tokenization across different languages, likely showing how the same concept requires more tokens in non-English languages (e.g., Thai, Chinese, Arabic) compared to English, illustrating token inefficiency for non-Latin scripts.
teaching_value: Helps students understand why LLMs trained primarily on English data are less efficient and effective for other languages — non-English text often requires more tokens to represent the same meaning, increasing cost and reducing context window capacity.
when_to_show: Surface this when teaching why building LLMs for non-English languages is a distinct engineering challenge, or when discussing tokenizer design and its downstream effects on model performance and cost.
suggested_caption: Non-English tokenization is less efficient, inflating token counts. (huyenchip.com)

description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how multi-head attention parallelizes attention across subspaces, helping students understand why splitting into heads enables richer representation than single attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, before or alongside the mathematical formulation, to ground the equations in a concrete computational flow.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.
teaching_value: Provides a complete structural overview of the Transformer, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact, where each component sits, and how information flows end-to-end.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson as a reference map, and revisit when discussing individual components to show where they fit in the full architecture.
suggested_caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract sinusoidal encoding formula concrete by showing how position information is encoded as a 2D pattern, helping students intuit why nearby positions have similar encodings and how frequency varies with dimension.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the self-attention permutation-invariance problem and before or alongside the sinusoidal formula, to help students visualize what the encoding actually looks like.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.

description: Diagram illustrating Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE): shows how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position.
teaching_value: Clarifies the geometric intuition behind RoPE — that relative position is encoded as a rotation — helping students understand why it generalizes better than additive positional encodings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced positional encoding methods, after students understand sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs. rotational approaches.
suggested_caption: RoPE encodes position by rotating token embeddings in 2D subspaces. (Su et al., 2021)

description: Side-by-side comparison of training in vanilla Transformer vs. Transformer-XL with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.
teaching_value: Directly illustrates the key innovation of Transformer-XL — segment-level recurrence — by contrasting it with the fixed-context limitation of vanilla Transformer, making the concept of extended context tangible.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and introducing Transformer-XL as a solution, to make the architectural difference immediately visible.
suggested_caption: Transformer-XL extends context by caching previous segment hidden states. (Dai et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory tiers: recent past stored as regular memory and older past compressed into compressed memory, with the model attending to both alongside current input.
teaching_value: Illustrates the two-level memory hierarchy of the Compressive Transformer, helping students understand how compression extends temporal range beyond Transformer-XL while managing memory cost.
when_to_show: Show after explaining Transformer-XL, when introducing the Compressive Transformer as a further extension, to highlight the added compressed memory tier.
suggested_caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right), comparing attention to regular memory vs. compressed memory in the Compressive Transformer.
teaching_value: Empirically demonstrates that the model attends meaningfully to compressed memory for older content, validating the compression approach and helping students understand the tradeoff between recency and compression.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the Compressive Transformer architecture, as empirical evidence that compressed memory is actually used by the model and not ignored.
suggested_caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of SPALM combining short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL style cache) with long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore), using a gating mechanism to merge both memory sources.
teaching_value: Shows how differentiable and non-differentiable memory can be combined in a single model, helping students understand hybrid memory architectures and the role of gating in balancing local vs. retrieved context.
when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, after k-NN LM, to illustrate how retrieval-augmented memory integrates with recurrent hidden states.
suggested_caption: SPALM gates short-term hidden states with long-term k-NN memory. (Yogatama et al., 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing perplexity of vanilla Transformer, Memorizing Transformer trained from scratch, and a vanilla Transformer fine-tuned with k-NN memory, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance to training from scratch.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that k-NN memory augmentation can be added via fine-tuning rather than full retraining, helping students understand the practical efficiency of the Memorizing Transformer approach.
when_to_show: Show when discussing the Memorizing Transformer to emphasize its practical advantage: existing pretrained models can be augmented with memory without full retraining.
suggested_caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches training Memorizing Transformer from scratch. (Wu et al., 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases): shows how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.
teaching_value: Makes the ALiBi mechanism concrete by showing exactly where and how the distance-based penalty is applied to attention scores, helping students understand how it encodes relative position without learned embeddings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, after introducing the motivation for relative positional encoding, to contrast ALiBi's simple linear bias with more complex approaches like RoPE.
suggested_caption: ALiBi adds a per-head linear distance penalty to attention scores. (Press et al., 2021)

description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve, indicating best extrapolation.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates why ALiBi's linear distance penalty enables length generalization while other encodings degrade sharply, making the abstract concept of 'extrapolation' concrete and comparable across methods.
when_to_show: Show after introducing ALiBi and before or during comparison with other positional encoding schemes, especially when students ask why ALiBi was proposed or what problem it solves.
suggested_caption: ALiBi extrapolates to longer contexts better than sinusoidal, rotary, or T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)

description: Animated-style diagram showing how Universal Transformer repeatedly applies the same transformation to all positions in parallel across multiple recurrent steps, with arrows indicating iterative refinement of hidden states h^1 through h^T.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key innovation of Universal Transformer — depth is recurrent and adaptive rather than fixed — helping students distinguish it from standard Transformers and understand how RNN-like recurrence is incorporated.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer, particularly to contrast with standard multi-layer Transformers and to illustrate how recurrence operates over depth rather than sequence position.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.
teaching_value: Shows the architectural symmetry between encoder and decoder in Universal Transformer and highlights the cross-attention connection, helping students map the abstract recurrence equations to a concrete architecture diagram.
when_to_show: Show after the recurrence loop diagram (Image 2) to give students the full encoder-decoder picture, especially when discussing sequence-to-sequence tasks or comparing to standard Transformer architecture.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent structure and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)

description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z(x) used in adaptive attention span, showing a smooth sigmoid-like curve that transitions from 1 to 0 around the learned parameter z, with the x-axis representing distance and y-axis the mask value in [0,1].
teaching_value: Makes the mathematical definition of the soft mask function tangible — students can see how the function smoothly gates attention weights based on distance, enabling gradient-based learning of the attention span parameter z.
when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize how the piecewise linear function controls the effective attention span and why it is differentiable.
suggested_caption: Soft mask function smoothly gates attention by distance, enabling learnable span z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)

description: Three-panel illustration comparing sequence-specific, token-specific, and token-and-label-specific depth classifiers in Depth-Adaptive Transformer, showing how exit decisions are made at different granularities across layers.
teaching_value: Concretely distinguishes three classifier strategies for early exit, helping students understand the trade-off between computational savings and prediction granularity in adaptive depth models.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Depth-Adaptive Transformer and comparing it to CALM, or when discussing the design space of early-exit strategies in efficient inference.
suggested_caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-, token-, and token-label-specific exits. (Elabyad et al. 2020)

description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image pixels in Image Transformer. Black outlines mark query blocks; cyan outlines show the actual attention span for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted locally.
teaching_value: Visually explains how local attention windows are defined differently for 1D (raster-scan) vs 2D (spatial) image layouts, making the abstract notion of 'local context' concrete for visual data.
when_to_show: Show when introducing fixed local context attention, especially to contrast 1D sequence-based and 2D spatial attention patterns, or when discussing how Transformers are adapted for image generation.
suggested_caption: 1D and 2D local attention spans for image pixels in Image Transformer. (Parmar et al. 2018)

description: Six-panel figure with top row showing attention connectivity graphs for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention, and bottom row showing corresponding sparse attention matrices.
teaching_value: Directly compares full vs. two sparse attention patterns both as graphs and as matrices, enabling students to see how sparsity is structured and how strided vs. fixed patterns differ in which positions attend to which.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Sparse Transformer to contrast with full self-attention, and to help students understand the structural difference between strided and fixed sparse patterns.
suggested_caption: Full, strided, and fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)

description: Three-panel diagram showing attention patterns for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird models, each illustrating how global tokens interact with all positions while local tokens use windowed attention, with Big Bird also including random attention.
teaching_value: Allows direct visual comparison of three global-local attention architectures, making it easy to identify shared design principles (global tokens + local windows) and unique additions like Big Bird's random attention component.
when_to_show: Show when comparing ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird to help students identify commonalities and differences, or as a summary visual after covering all three models.
suggested_caption: Attention patterns of ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird: global tokens plus local windows.

description: Matrix visualization of LSH attention showing how queries and keys are grouped into hash buckets, sorted, and chunked, with the resulting sparse attention matrix highlighting which positions attend to each other within the same bucket.
teaching_value: Shows the resulting sparsity structure of LSH attention as a matrix, helping students understand which query-key pairs are computed and why the pattern is content-dependent rather than fixed.
when_to_show: Show alongside the LSH process diagram (Image 10) to connect the algorithmic steps to the resulting attention matrix structure, especially when students struggle to see why LSH produces valid approximate attention.
suggested_caption: LSH attention matrix: queries attend only within the same hash bucket. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Step-by-step process diagram of LSH attention showing four stages: (1) hashing queries and keys into buckets, (2) sorting by bucket, (3) chunking into fixed-size segments, and (4) computing attention within chunks.
teaching_value: Breaks down the LSH attention algorithm into discrete, visually trackable steps, making it easier for students to understand how approximate nearest-neighbor search is implemented efficiently within the attention mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing LSH attention in Reformer, walking through each step sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
suggested_caption: LSH attention: four steps — bucketing, sorting, chunking, attention computation. (Kitaev et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Linformer architecture diagram showing linear projection layers E_i and F_i added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) Log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer vs. standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how low-rank projection reduces the quadratic attention bottleneck to linear complexity, and the right panel provides empirical evidence of the computational speedup at long sequence lengths.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Linformer after explaining the quadratic cost of standard attention; use the right panel to motivate why linear approximations matter at scale.
suggested_caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel diagram comparing computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computing the full L×L attention matrix before weighting values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reordering operations to compute φ(Q)(φ(K)^T V) without materializing the full matrix, enabling linear complexity.
teaching_value: Illustrates the kernel trick insight behind RFA — by reordering matrix multiplications using random feature maps, the expensive L×L attention matrix is never explicitly formed, making the computational savings concrete and intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel methods enable linear-time attention; use the side-by-side to make the reordering trick tangible before introducing the φ transformation formula.
suggested_caption: RFA reorders computation to avoid the full attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)

description: Two-panel plot comparing approximation error: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing orthogonal features have lower variance/error; (Right) sin/cos (signed) random features vs. positive random features showing positive features better approximate the softmax kernel.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for two key design choices in the Performer model — using orthogonal rather than i.i.d. features reduces approximation error, and positive random features are necessary for stable softmax kernel approximation.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the RFA framework when discussing Performer's improvements; use to justify why orthogonal and positive feature choices are not arbitrary but empirically superior.
suggested_caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)

description: Three-column architecture diagram comparing: (1) Transformer-XL with standard layer norm placement; (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using reordered layer norm, enabling stable RL training.
teaching_value: Makes the two specific architectural modifications of GTrXL visually explicit — layer norm reordering and gating units — helping students understand how each change addresses training instability in the RL setting.
when_to_show: Show when introducing GTrXL after discussing why standard Transformers are hard to train in RL; use to walk through each modification and connect it to the stability motivation.
suggested_caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)

description: Diagram from Bahdanau et al. (2014) illustrating the attention mechanism applied to a recurrent neural network for neural machine translation, showing how alignment scores connect encoder hidden states to decoder outputs.
teaching_value: Concretely shows how attention allows a decoder to selectively focus on different parts of the input sequence, motivating why attention was needed before the full Transformer was developed.
when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the historical motivation for attention — before presenting the Transformer — to show how RNNs struggled with long-range dependencies and how attention addressed that.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau et al. (2014): attention mechanism for RNN-based machine translation.

description: The original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram from 'Attention Is All You Need' (Vaswani et al., 2017), showing stacked encoder and decoder blocks with multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, positional encoding, and residual connections.
teaching_value: Provides the canonical visual reference for the full Transformer architecture, allowing students to identify each component (multi-head attention, positional encoding, feed-forward sublayers) and understand how encoder and decoder interact.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the Transformer architecture; use as a reference diagram throughout discussions of attention, layer normalization placement, and encoder vs. decoder variants.
suggested_caption: Vaswani et al. (2017): original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram.

description: Side-by-side comparison of three Transformer sublayer configurations (1456×682): the original Post-LN Transformer from 'Attention Is All You Need' (left), an intermediate variant (center), and the Pre-LN Transformer from Xiong et al. (2020) (right), highlighting where layer normalization is placed relative to residual connections.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the difference between Post-LN and Pre-LN Transformer variants, helping students understand why layer normalization placement matters for gradient stability and why most modern architectures adopt Pre-LN.
when_to_show: Show when discussing layer normalization choices in Transformer design, especially when students ask why modern implementations differ from the original paper's figure.
suggested_caption: Post-LN vs. Pre-LN Transformer: layer normalization placement comparison (Vaswani 2017; Xiong 2020).

description: Annotated diagram from Schmidhuber (1991) illustrating the fast-weight programmer concept, showing how a slow network generates weight updates for a fast network — an early precursor to the self-attention mechanism in Transformers.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the deep historical roots of Transformer-like mechanisms, helping students appreciate that self-attention has conceptual predecessors from the 1990s and understand the connection to linear Transformers and fast-weight programmers.
when_to_show: Surface when discussing the theoretical foundations or history of attention mechanisms, particularly when covering linear Transformers or when students ask about the origins of self-attention.
suggested_caption: Schmidhuber (1991): fast-weight programmer — an early precursor to Transformer self-attention.

description: Diagram from Howard & Ruder (2018) ULMFiT paper (1456×702) illustrating the three-stage transfer learning pipeline: (1) language model pretraining on a large corpus, (2) language model fine-tuning on target data, and (3) classifier fine-tuning with gradual layer unfreezing.
teaching_value: Illustrates the pretrain-then-finetune paradigm that became the foundation for BERT, GPT, and modern LLMs, helping students understand why this recipe is so powerful and how catastrophic forgetting is mitigated.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the pretrain-finetune paradigm, before or alongside BERT and GPT discussions, to establish the conceptual recipe that all subsequent foundation models follow.
suggested_caption: Howard & Ruder (2018): ULMFiT three-stage pretrain-then-finetune transfer learning pipeline.

description: Architecture diagram from Devlin et al. (2018) BERT paper, showing the bidirectional Transformer encoder with masked language modeling (MLM) and next sentence prediction (NSP) pretraining objectives, and how BERT embeddings are applied to downstream tasks.
teaching_value: Shows how BERT uses the encoder-only Transformer with bidirectional context, contrasting with GPT's unidirectional decoder approach, helping students understand the encoder vs. decoder design split in LLM research.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the encoder-style LLM branch, directly contrasting with the GPT decoder-style diagram to highlight the architectural and objective differences.
suggested_caption: Devlin et al. (2018): BERT bidirectional encoder with masked language model pretraining.

description: Architecture and task diagram from the original GPT paper (Radford & Narasimhan, 2018), illustrating the decoder-only Transformer with unidirectional (left-to-right) autoregressive language modeling pretraining and task-specific fine-tuning heads.
teaching_value: Contrasts with BERT by showing the decoder-only, unidirectional autoregressive design that underlies GPT-2, GPT-3, and ChatGPT, helping students understand why this architecture dominates generative LLMs today.
when_to_show: Show alongside the BERT diagram when teaching the encoder vs. decoder split; emphasize that this architecture is the foundation of modern generative LLMs including ChatGPT.
suggested_caption: Radford & Narasimhan (2018): GPT decoder-only autoregressive Transformer architecture.

description: Architecture diagram from Lewis et al. (2019) BART paper, showing the sequence-to-sequence denoising pretraining approach that combines a bidirectional encoder with an autoregressive decoder, along with various corruption/noising strategies applied to input text.
teaching_value: Illustrates how BART unifies encoder and decoder strengths by using denoising as a pretraining objective, helping students understand sequence-to-sequence models and how they bridge the BERT/GPT divide for generation and comprehension tasks.
when_to_show: Show after covering BERT and GPT to illustrate how encoder-decoder models like BART synthesize both approaches; useful when discussing summarization, translation, and comprehension tasks.
suggested_caption: Lewis et al. (2019): BART denoising sequence-to-sequence pretraining architecture.

description: An evolutionary tree (phylogenetic-style diagram) showing the lineage and branching of modern LLMs, distinguishing encoder-only (BERT-style), decoder-only (GPT-style), and encoder-decoder architectures over time.
teaching_value: Gives students a visual timeline of how LLM architectures diverged and evolved, making it easy to see which models share design lineage and how the field progressed from early transformers to modern LLMs.
when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on LLM architectures to orient students before diving into specific model families; use it as a reference map when introducing any individual model.
suggested_caption: Evolutionary tree of modern LLMs showing encoder, decoder, and hybrid lineages. (Yang et al., 2023)

description: A diagram or figure from the FlashAttention paper (Dao et al., 2022) illustrating the IO-aware attention mechanism, likely showing GPU memory hierarchy (HBM vs. SRAM) and how tiled computation reduces memory reads/writes.
teaching_value: Helps students understand why standard attention is memory-bandwidth-bound and how FlashAttention restructures computation to minimize slow HBM accesses, making the efficiency gain concrete and intuitive.
when_to_show: Introduce when teaching attention mechanism optimizations or when students ask why standard attention doesn't scale well to long sequences.
suggested_caption: FlashAttention reduces memory bottlenecks via IO-aware tiled attention computation. (Dao et al., 2022)

description: A figure from the 'Cramming' paper showing training dynamics or throughput trade-offs when training a BERT-style masked language model on a single GPU for 24 hours, likely comparing model size vs. training efficiency or performance thresholds.
teaching_value: Illustrates the counterintuitive insight that smaller models have higher throughput but learn less efficiently per parameter, helping students reason about the trade-off between model size and training budget.
when_to_show: Use when discussing practical constraints of LLM training or when introducing scaling laws; pairs well with Chinchilla scaling law discussion.
suggested_caption: Training BERT on one GPU in 24h reveals size-efficiency trade-offs. (Geiping & Goldstein, 2022)

description: A two-part illustration: (left) the LoRA mechanism showing how weight updates ΔW are decomposed into two low-rank matrices A and B added alongside frozen pretrained weights; (right) a performance comparison table or chart showing LoRA matches or exceeds full fine-tuning with far fewer trainable parameters.
teaching_value: Makes the mathematical intuition of low-rank decomposition visually concrete and immediately shows students that parameter efficiency does not sacrifice task performance, motivating why LoRA is widely adopted.
when_to_show: Show when introducing fine-tuning strategies, especially after explaining why full fine-tuning of large models is computationally prohibitive.
suggested_caption: LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices, enabling efficient fine-tuning. (Hu et al., 2021)

description: A taxonomy or landscape diagram from the parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) survey, categorizing various PEFT methods (e.g., adapter layers, prefix tuning, LoRA, prompt tuning) and their relationships.
teaching_value: Provides students with a structured overview of the PEFT landscape so they can situate LoRA within a broader family of methods and understand the design space of fine-tuning approaches.
when_to_show: Show after introducing LoRA to broaden the student's view of fine-tuning options; useful for students who need to choose a PEFT method for a specific task.
suggested_caption: Taxonomy of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods beyond LoRA. (Lialin et al., 2022)

description: A figure from the Gopher paper showing architecture details or training analysis of the 280B parameter Gopher model, likely illustrating normalization choices (RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm) or training stability metrics across model scales.
teaching_value: Concretely demonstrates architectural decisions made when scaling LLMs to hundreds of billions of parameters, helping students understand why RMSNorm is preferred over LayerNorm in deep distributed training settings.
when_to_show: Use when teaching normalization layers in transformers or when discussing engineering decisions that matter at scale; good contrast point between theory and practice.
suggested_caption: Gopher's 280B model uses RMSNorm for improved training stability at scale. (Rae et al., 2022)

description: A figure from the Chinchilla paper showing compute-optimal scaling laws, likely a plot of model size vs. training tokens with iso-compute curves, demonstrating that optimal performance requires balancing model size and dataset size rather than maximizing model parameters alone.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the Chinchilla scaling law insight that most LLMs are undertrained — students can see that doubling model size should be matched by doubling training tokens, fundamentally changing how to allocate a compute budget.
when_to_show: Show when introducing scaling laws; use to challenge the assumption that bigger models are always better and to motivate data-centric approaches to LLM training.
suggested_caption: Chinchilla scaling laws show LLMs are undertrained; tokens matter as much as parameters. (Hoffmann et al., 2022)

description: An overview figure of the Pythia model suite showing the range of open-source models (70M to 12B parameters) trained on the same data with checkpoints saved throughout training, enabling controlled comparisons across scale and training duration.
teaching_value: Illustrates how a controlled model suite enables scientific study of LLM training dynamics, helping students understand the value of open, reproducible research infrastructure for understanding scaling and data effects.
when_to_show: Use when discussing open-source LLM research infrastructure or when teaching about controlled experiments in ML; also relevant when discussing the effect of training data repetition.
suggested_caption: Pythia suite: 8 open LLMs from 70M–12B for studying training and scaling. (Biderman et al., 2023)

description: A three-step diagram from the InstructGPT paper illustrating the RLHF pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs, (2) training a reward model from human preference rankings, and (3) optimizing the language model with PPO using the reward model.
teaching_value: Makes the RLHF process concrete and sequential, allowing students to understand each component's role — supervised learning, reward modeling, and reinforcement learning — and how they combine to align LLM outputs with human intent.
when_to_show: Show when introducing alignment techniques or RLHF; essential visual for any lesson on how ChatGPT-style models are trained to follow instructions safely.
suggested_caption: InstructGPT's 3-step RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, then PPO optimization. (Ouyang et al., 2022)

description: A diagram from the Constitutional AI paper illustrating the CAI pipeline, likely showing how an AI model critiques and revises its own outputs according to a set of principles (the 'constitution'), replacing human feedback with AI-generated feedback for harmlessness training.
teaching_value: Shows students an alternative to human-labeled RLHF that scales better, introducing the concept of AI self-supervision for alignment and the role of explicit principles in guiding model behavior.
when_to_show: Show after RLHF (Image 9) to contrast human feedback with AI-generated feedback; useful for discussions on scalable alignment and reducing reliance on human annotators.
suggested_caption: Constitutional AI uses AI self-critique to train harmless models without human labels. (Bai et al., 2022)

description: Annotated flowchart of the Self-Instruct pipeline showing four stages: (1) generating task instructions from a seed set using a pretrained LLM, (2) classifying task category, (3) generating input/output instances, and (4) filtering low-quality examples before adding to the instruction pool for fine-tuning.
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete visual of how synthetic instruction datasets are bootstrapped from a pretrained LLM without human labeling, making the abstract loop of self-improvement tangible and showing exactly where human effort is replaced by automation.
when_to_show: Show when introducing instruction-tuning alternatives to human-labeled datasets, specifically when explaining how models like Alpaca were trained cheaply at scale.
suggested_caption: Self-Instruct pipeline: bootstrapping instruction data with a pretrained LLM. (Raschka, adapted from Wang et al. 2022)

description: Full Transformer encoder–decoder architecture diagram showing stacked encoder layers (self-attention + feed-forward with residual connections and layer normalization), stacked decoder layers (masked self-attention, encoder–decoder cross-attention, feed-forward), plus input/output embeddings, positional encodings, and final linear + softmax output.
teaching_value: Helps learners map the end-to-end dataflow of a Transformer, distinguishing encoder vs decoder roles, where self-attention vs cross-attention occurs, and how residual connections/normalization wrap each sublayer.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer at a high level, or when a student confuses encoder self-attention with decoder cross-attention; use it to trace one token’s path from input embedding to output probabilities.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder–decoder architecture overview (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Figure 2 composite: left panel illustrates scaled dot-product attention as Q, K, V matrices multiplied (QK^T), scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k), passed through softmax to produce attention weights, then multiplied by V; right panel shows multi-head attention with multiple parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Makes the attention computation concrete as matrix operations and clarifies why scaling and softmax are used; also shows how multi-head attention increases representational capacity by attending to different subspaces in parallel.
when_to_show: Show when deriving the attention formula or implementing attention; use it to explain tensor shapes (Q,K,V) and to justify multi-head attention vs a single attention head.
suggested_caption: Scaled dot-product and multi-head attention schematic (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Larger rendering of Figure 2 emphasizing the multi-head attention block: separate linear projections create per-head Q, K, V; each head performs scaled dot-product attention; head outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear layer.
teaching_value: Supports step-by-step explanation of how heads are formed via learned projections and recombined, helping students understand implementation details beyond the single-head attention equation.
when_to_show: Show after introducing scaled dot-product attention, when moving to multi-head attention implementation details (e.g., splitting/reshaping tensors and combining heads).
suggested_caption: Multi-head attention computation pipeline (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Attention visualization for the encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6) focusing on the token “making”; colored lines/weights from “making” to other tokens show multiple heads attending strongly to a distant phrase completion (“making … more difficult”).
teaching_value: Demonstrates that self-attention can capture long-range dependencies without recurrence, giving an intuitive, visual explanation of how different heads link distant but syntactically/semantically related words.
when_to_show: Show when a student asks how Transformers handle long-range context, or when contrasting self-attention with RNNs for dependency tracking; use it to discuss head diversity.
suggested_caption: Encoder self-attention links distant dependency for “making” (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Anaphora-related attention visualization: top shows full attention pattern for a specific head (head 5) across tokens; bottom isolates attention originating from the word “its” for heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused links to likely antecedents.
teaching_value: Helps learners see that some heads learn coreference/anaphora behaviors, and illustrates the idea of sharp vs diffuse attention distributions as an interpretable signal.
when_to_show: Show when teaching interpretability of attention heads or discussing whether attention can reflect linguistic structure; use it to explain how pronouns may attend to antecedents.
suggested_caption: Attention heads focusing on “its” for anaphora-like links (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Second panel associated with Figure 4: additional attention heatmaps/links for heads 5 and 6, emphasizing isolated attention from the token “its” and the sharpness of the resulting attention peaks.
teaching_value: Provides another view of head-specific attention patterns, reinforcing that different heads can encode different linguistic relations and that attention can be highly selective for certain tokens.
when_to_show: Show alongside the first anaphora figure when comparing multiple heads’ behavior on the same token; useful for exercises interpreting attention maps.
suggested_caption: Isolated attention from “its” across two heads (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 1 from encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6), showing one head’s attention aligning tokens in a pattern consistent with syntactic structure (e.g., phrase boundaries or dependency-like links).
teaching_value: Shows that attention heads can learn structured patterns resembling syntax, helping students connect abstract attention weights to recognizable linguistic organization.
when_to_show: Show when discussing emergent linguistic structure in Transformers or when students need intuition for why multiple heads are useful beyond redundancy.
suggested_caption: Example head exhibiting sentence-structure-like attention (Vaswani et al., 2017).

description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 2 from a different encoder self-attention head (layer 5 of 6), contrasting with the previous example to show a different learned attention pattern/task.
teaching_value: Supports comparison across heads to illustrate division of labor: different heads attend to different relationships, reinforcing the motivation for multi-head attention.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the other sentence-structure example to prompt comparison (What differs? What might each head be tracking?).
suggested_caption: Another head with distinct structure-related attention (Vaswani et al., 2017).
